title: The day I finally said his name right
summary: A short story about the day you finally pronounced someone's name correctly after months of trying.
topic: The day I finally said his name right
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences (150–250 words) about a name you struggled to pronounce until you finally got it right. Describe the person, the long effort, and the moment it worked. Use past simple and used to.
hintStarters: For almost four months I worked next to a man whose name was…
I used to say it as 'Mateus' until one of his cousins corrected me…
On the day of his birthday lunch, I finally said it the way…
sampleText: For almost four months I worked next to a man at a small printing shop in Quito whose name was Mateo. The problem was that I kept calling him 'Mateus', the Portuguese version, because my last job had been in Brazil. I used to say it wrong twenty times a day, and he used to smile at me every time, never correcting it. One Wednesday his cousin came in with lunch and laughed at me. 'It is Ma-TE-o,' she said. 'Three soft syllables, like a small song.' I practised quietly all afternoon. I whispered it while I was wrapping packages and while I was washing my coffee cup. The next morning I came in, looked at him, and said clearly, 'Buenos días, Mateo.' He stopped what he was doing, put down his cutter, and grinned. 'Finally,' he said. He was not angry, only happy. On the day of his birthday lunch the following week, his mother held my arm and thanked me. I had not understood until then that getting a name right is a small kind of respect that nobody can buy for you. You only earn it by trying, and by listening more than you speak.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Tôi làm việc bên cạnh một người đàn ông ở một tiệm in nhỏ
Tôi cứ gọi anh ấy là 'Mateus'

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I used to say it wrong twenty times a day

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: anh ấy đã hay mỉm cười với tôi mỗi lần

sampleAnnotations.phrase: He stopped what he was doing, put down his cutter, and grinned
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Anh ấy dừng việc đang làm, đặt con dao rọc xuống, và nhe răng cười

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'Used to + base verb' for past habit
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I used to say it wrong' và 'he used to smile at me' dùng 'used to + V' cho thói quen quá khứ không còn diễn ra. Sau 'used to' luôn là động từ nguyên mẫu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I used to say it wrong' and 'he used to smile at me' use 'used to + base verb' for a past habit that no longer happens. The base verb always follows 'used to'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect for prior background
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'my last job had been in Brazil' dùng past perfect ('had been') để nói về một thời kỳ kết thúc trước câu chuyện chính ở quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'My last job had been in Brazil' uses past perfect ('had been') to refer to a period that ended before the main past story.

sampleTranslationVi: Trong gần bốn tháng, tôi làm việc bên cạnh một người đàn ông ở một tiệm in nhỏ tại Quito tên là Mateo. Vấn đề là tôi cứ gọi anh ấy là 'Mateus', phiên bản Bồ Đào Nha, vì công việc trước đó của tôi đã từng ở Brazil. Tôi đã hay nói sai hai mươi lần mỗi ngày, và anh ấy đã hay mỉm cười với tôi mỗi lần, không bao giờ sửa. Một thứ Tư, em họ anh ấy mang bữa trưa đến và bật cười với tôi. 'Là Ma-TE-o,' cô ấy nói. 'Ba âm tiết nhẹ, như một bài hát nhỏ.' Tôi luyện tập âm thầm cả buổi chiều. Tôi thì thầm nó trong khi đang gói các kiện hàng và trong khi đang rửa cốc cà phê. Sáng hôm sau, tôi đến, nhìn anh ấy, và nói rõ ràng, 'Buenos días, Mateo.' Anh ấy dừng việc đang làm, đặt con dao rọc xuống, và nhe răng cười. 'Cuối cùng cũng được,' anh ấy nói. Anh ấy không giận, chỉ vui. Vào ngày bữa trưa sinh nhật của anh ấy tuần sau đó, mẹ anh ấy nắm tay tôi và cảm ơn tôi. Mãi đến lúc đó tôi mới hiểu rằng gọi đúng một cái tên là một dạng tôn trọng nhỏ mà không ai có thể mua hộ bạn. Bạn chỉ có được nó bằng cách cố gắng, và bằng cách lắng nghe nhiều hơn nói.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence with 'used to'.
options: I used to said it wrong twenty times a day.
I use to say it wrong twenty times a day.
I am used to say it wrong twenty times a day.
I used to say it wrong twenty times a day.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' for a past habit.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct past perfect sentence.
options: My last job had been in Brazil.
My last job has been in Brazil.
My last job was been in Brazil.
My last job had being in Brazil.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had been' for a period before another past time.

options: the meaning of a word
a unit of sound in a word, like the 'ma' in 'mateo'
the spelling of a word
a special letter
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Syllable' = a unit of pronunciation containing a vowel sound.

options: I kept to call him 'Mateus'.
I kept call him 'Mateus'.
I kept calling him 'Mateus'.
I have kept call him 'Mateus'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct 'while' + past continuous sentence.
options: I whispered it while I wrapping packages.
I whispered it while I wrap packages.
I was whispered it while I wrapping packages.
I whispered it while I was wrapping packages.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past continuous 'was wrapping' for the ongoing background; past simple 'whispered' for the action.

options: to smile widely showing the teeth
to look very serious
to look very tired
to cry quietly
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Grin' = a wide smile, usually showing teeth.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct past simple sentence.
options: He stop what he was doing, put down his cutter, and grinned.
He stopped what he was doing, put down his cutter, and grinned.
He stopped what he doing, put down his cutter, and grinned.
He stopped what he was do, put down his cutter, and grinned.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple chain 'stopped, put, grinned'; past continuous 'was doing' in the subordinate clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence with 'thank for / thank + object'.
options: His mother held my arm and thanked to me.
His mother held my arm and thanked at me.
His mother held my arm and thanked me.
His mother held my arm and thank me.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence with 'until then'.
options: I had not understood until then that getting a name right is a small kind of respect.
I did not understand until then that getting a name right is a small kind of respect.
I have not understood until then that getting a name right is a small kind of respect.
Both A and B are correct.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both past perfect and past simple work here; past perfect emphasises the moment of realisation.

options: Both A and B are correct.
Getting a name right is a small kind of respect.
To get a name right is a small kind of respect.
Get a name right is a small kind of respect.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both gerund and infinitive can be subject, but gerund is more natural in modern English.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural closing sentence.
options: You only earn it by try, and by listen more than you speak.
You only earn it by trying, and by listening more than you speak.
You only earn it for trying, and for listening more than you speak.
You only earn it to try, and to listen more than you speak.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'By + -ing' is the standard pattern for means.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why does getting someone's name right feel different from any other polite gesture? What does it actually communicate to them?
